mcqs.explanation: The while loop executes as long as i < 3, printing values 0, 1, and 2. When i becomes 3, the condition fails and the loop terminates.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is the correct syntax for a for loop in C++ to iterate from 1 to 5 (both inclusive)?

options.text: for (int i = 1; i < 5; i++)
for (int i = 0; i <= 5; i++)
for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++)
for (int i = 1; i > 5; i++)

mcqs.explanation: Starting from 1 and using i <= 5 ensures the loop runs for values 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 (5 iterations).

mcqs.question: What is the key difference between a while loop and a do-while loop in C++?

options.text: while loop is faster than do-while loop
do-while loop always executes at least once, while while loop may not execute at all
while loop can only be used with integers
There is no difference; they are interchangeable

mcqs.explanation: The do-while loop tests the condition after executing the loop body, guaranteeing at least one execution, unlike the while loop which checks the condition first.
The continue statement skips the current iteration when j == 1, so pairs with j=1 are skipped (01, 11, 21), printing only 00, 02, 10, 12, 20, 22.

options.text: Missing semicolon after sum declaration
Loop condition should be i <= 10 instead of i < 10
sum should be initialized to 1
i++ should be ++i

mcqs.explanation: The condition i < 10 stops at 9, missing the number 10. It should be i <= 10 to include all numbers from 1 to 10.

options.text: Version A is more efficient because ++i returns a value while i++ creates a temporary copy
Version B is more efficient because post-increment is optimized by modern compilers
Both are equally efficient; modern compilers optimize both to the same assembly code
Version B is more efficient because it doesn't modify the original variable

mcqs.explanation: In modern C++ with optimizations enabled, both versions generate identical assembly code. Compilers perform return value optimization (RVO) eliminating any difference.
The break only exits the inner loop when i == j. For i=0: inner runs 0 times (break immediately). i=1: j=0 executes once. i=2: j=0,1 execute twice. i=3: j=0,1,2 execute thrice. Total = 0+1+2+3 = 6.

options.text: Both loops will always terminate
The first loop may infinite if i is a floating-point number due to precision errors
The second loop will always be infinite because j starts at 0 and decreases
Both loops will be infinite due to integer overflow

mcqs.explanation: If i were a float (e.g., float i = 10.0f), decrementing by 1.0f repeatedly may not reach exactly 0.0f due to floating-point precision, causing the loop to run indefinitely.
